# Why Do We Need Experimental Design? {#sec-why-design}

Let's justify why we need to consider experimental design and power calculations at all, but especially in the context of animals in research.

::: { .column-margin }
![Twelve Monkeys? Are you sure you need twelve? Why not eight? Or fifteen?](assets/images/twelve_monkeys.jpg){#fig-twelve-monkeys}
:::

## We should not cause unnecessary suffering

No-one likes to talk about animal experiments. It's a difficult, emotive topic that crosses many people's moral red lines. But still, animal experiments are sometimes the only practical way to gain scientific knowledge. Sometimes those experiments require that animals experience distress, discomfort or pain, or that they are sacrificed or euthanised. Whatever you believe "suffering" means for an animal, we should not allow that suffering to be in vain.

::: { .callout-warning title="The Moral Axiom" }
**We should always minimise suffering.**

This might mean not performing an experiment at all: it is not always the case that a new piece of knowledge or understanding is _worth_ causing suffering to obtain it. But when it is considered that there _is_ sufficient justification for performing an experiment, we are morally obliged to minimise the amount of distress or suffering that is caused.
:::

It's relatively easy to tell whether an animal is being well-treated, or whether an experiment is necessary. But it's not that easy to tell whether research using animals causes the _right_ (i.e. _least possible_) amount of suffering.

::: { .callout-question }
:::::{.question}
Suppose you are running a experiment with animal subjects, where the outcome of the experiment is useful and necessary, and the use of animals is morally justified. **Which of the following choices will cause the _least amount of suffering_?**

::::{.choices}

:::{.choice}
Using just enough subjects per group to calculate a standard deviation (i.e. three) and perform a _t_-test.
:::  

:::{.choice .correct-choice}
Using just enough subjects to establish that the experimental outcome is very likely to be the correct result.
:::

:::{.choice}
Using just enough subjects that the experimental outcome is certain to be the correct result.
:::

:::{.choice}
Using as many subjects as you have available out of convenience.
:::

:::{.button-clear title="Clear answer" button-class="btn btn-xs"}
:::

::::

::::{.btn-group}
:::{.button-hint title="Show hint" button-class="btn btn-xs"}
What is the purpose of performing the experiment, and how certain do you need to be?
:::

:::{.button-answer title="Show Answer" button-class="btn btn-xs"}
We carry out experiments to obtain answers to our scientific hypotheses, but the answers we obtain are rarely 100% certain. We usually aim to obtain answers that are very likely to be correct (think about what a statistical hypothesis test means: that the explanation is more or less likely to be the null hypothesis), rather than 100% certain.

If we use too few subjects we may still be able to perform a statistical test, but the results of the experiment will be uncertain and may be more likely to be incorrect than correct. **The use of animal subjects in an experiment that is unlikely to give a correct answer causes unnecessary suffering**.

If we attempt to obtain a 100% certain result we may need to use many more subjects - possibly tens or hundreds more - than are required to obtain a result at (say) 80% certainty. **The use of animal subjects to obtain a level of certainty greater than is needed to answer the question reasonably causes unnecessary suffering**.

If we use the number of subjects that are available, just because it is convenient, then we may not know how likely the experiment is to give us a correct answer. **The use of animal subjects in an experiment where you do not know how likely you are to get a correct answer causes unnecessary suffering**.
:::
::::

:::::
:::

::: { .callout-tip title="The Correct Number of Animal Subjects" collapse="true" }
The appropriate number of animal subjects to use in an experiment is _always_ the smallest number that - given reasonable assumptions - will satisfactorily give the correct result to the desired level of certainty.

**_By convention the usual level of certainty is: "that we have 80% chance of getting the correct yes/no answer for the hypothesis being tested"._**

Note though that the appropriate level of certainty may change depending on the nature of the question being asked.
:::

## Good Experimental Design Minimises Suffering And Produces Better Science

Aside from the question of using animal subjects, we should want all scientific research to be well conducted and well reported. Good experimental design is all part of conducting good science, as is reporting our experimental design and the results the experiment delivers clearly. This requirement is heightened when animal subjects are involved, because of the moral element of the research practice.

Experimental design and statistics work together to deliver sound, meaningful research that moves our collective understanding forward. Considering both of these before conducting research will improve your science.

### Experimental Design

Once a research hypothesis has been devised, _Experimental Design_ is the process by which the practical means of answering that question is constructed. The design should aim to exclude extraneous or confounding influences on the experiment such that the causal factors are isolated and measurable, and any difference in outcome as a result of changing those factors can also be measured cleanly.

### Statistics

All measurement is associated with _irreducible error_, experimental subjects come with their own inherent variability, and even the best experimental design often fails to exclude all possible external causes of variation. Statistics is the branch of applied science that allows us to make _probabilistic inferences_ about certainty of measurements and experimental outcomes.

::: { .callout-important title="What should I design my experiment for?"}
In general, you should design your experiment specifically for:

- your population or subject group (e.g. sex, age, prior history, etc.)
- your intervention (e.g. drug treatment)
- your contrast or comparison between groups (e.g. lung capacity, drug concentration, etc.)
- your outcome (i.e. is there a measurable or clinically relevant effect)
:::

::: { .callout-caution }
Does all published science follow good practice in experimental design and statistics?
:::
